text: Hello, my name is Ara Kashishian and I would like to review a robotic Lysol adhesion for a patient who presented to our office with a long standing abdominal pain. This had been worked up with CT scans which on one or two occasions had shown some dilated loops of bowel.

Multiple small incisions are visible on the patient's abdomen, through which trocars have been introduced. Robotic instruments are placed into the abdomen via these trocars, and the robot is connected for the procedure.
The robotic scissors are visible at the top of the image, with the tip bent to allow cutting at various angles without moving the instrument in and out of the patient.
As a surgeon I also have full control of the camera where I can toggle the control of the one of the four arms that has a camera to allow me to get closer and or further away and change the angle.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to dissect and cauterize scar tissue, detaching loops of bowel and surrounding fatty tissue from the anterior abdominal wall. Ultrasonic power is applied to control bleeding during the procedure.

The surgeon uses articulated scissors to dissect the small bowel from the abdominal wall, relieving the patient's partial bowel obstruction. The scissors are maneuvered through the same opening without moving in and out of the patient, showcasing their multi-axis articulation.
With laparoscopic approach, this robotic approach also allows us to do this with three or four different small incisions and they all heal relatively quickly.
The surgeon cauterizes a small area of bleeding, indicating the nearing completion of the adhesiolysis procedure.
